does: This paper develops an iterative post-deployment alignment framework for healthcare LLMs that combines Kahneman-Tversky Optimization and Direct Preference Optimization to refine model behavior using domain-specific safety signals. The authors evaluate four LLM variants on the CARES-18K adversarial robustness benchmark across multiple refinement cycles, achieving up to 42% improvement in harmful query detection metrics. The work characterizes critical trade-offs between safety and helpfulness, revealing architecture-dependent calibration biases that cause models to either over-refuse benign medical queries or under-refuse harmful ones. Through ablation studies, the researchers identify conditions under which self-evaluation succeeds versus when external or specialized judges are necessary to maximize performance gains in medical contexts.
connects: This work extends the line of research on preference-based model alignment exemplified by DPO and related techniques, adapting these methods from general-purpose domains to specialized healthcare settings where safety-critical decisions are required. It builds on prior work addressing the tension between model compliance and refusal—a challenge highlighted in recent research on constitutional AI and safety guidelines—but applies this specifically to medical assistants where both false positives (over-refusing legitimate clinical questions) and false negatives (missing harmful requests) carry concrete consequences. The paper responds to growing concerns about LLM deployment in regulated industries by integrating domain-specific adversarial evaluation benchmarks and examining how different model architectures and sizes interact with alignment procedures.
enables: This framework enables more responsible deployment of conversational medical assistants by providing methods to iteratively improve safety-utility trade-offs post-launch. The findings about architecture-dependent calibration biases suggest future work should investigate whether different alignment techniques or training procedures can reduce these inherent trade-offs. The ablation studies on judge reliability open avenues for developing efficient evaluation pipelines using combinations of automated and human assessment. More broadly, this work positions iterative alignment as a practical deployment strategy for high-stakes domains, pointing toward adaptive systems that can refine their behavior in response to domain-specific failure modes while maintaining clinical utility and user trust over time.
